title: Dealmaking in the Shadow of Hormuz: Russia-China Pipeline Advances as Ukraine Strikes Russian Energy
subhead: Power of Siberia-2 gains momentum, Ukraine escalates strikes on Russian refineries, and Europe bets on synthetic jet fuel as Hormuz reshapes energy security thinking.

sections.heading: Power of Siberia-2 Advances as Putin-Xi Align on Route
sections.body:
Following the May 20 summit between Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov announced a 'shared understanding on the main parameters of Power of Siberia-2' — specifically the route and construction approach, El Pais reports. The pipeline, planned to carry nearly 50 billion cubic meters of gas annually from Russia's Yamal Peninsula to Chinese demand centers, would complement Power of Siberia-1 (38 bcm/year) to cover roughly one-fifth of China's gas demand.

The urgency is asymmetric. Russia, cut off from European pipeline markets since the Ukraine invasion, desperately needs new export routes. China, facing Hormuz-driven LNG supply concerns, sees domestic pipeline gas as a hedge. The route through Mongolia has been finalized, with estimated costs above $11.5 billion. What remains unresolved: pricing formulas and construction timeline. The pipeline would run over 2,600 kilometers linking Arctic gas fields to Beijing and Shanghai.

According to Reuters, Kremlin officials said key details and timetables still need agreement. China has not publicly commented on progress, reflecting Beijing's bargaining advantage — it can wait while Moscow cannot.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia gains a critical outlet for sanctioned gas; China locks in overland supply that bypasses maritime chokepoints. The pipeline is structural — it locks in bilateral energy interdependence for decades.

sections.heading: Ukraine Escalates Energy Infrastructure Strikes Inside Russia
sections.body:
Ukrainian drones struck targets across several Russian regions overnight, including an oil pipeline pumping station, a refinery, and a fuel depot, Reuters reported Sunday. The strikes hit infrastructure deep inside Russian territory, part of an escalating campaign targeting energy facilities hundreds of kilometers from the front lines.

The attack follows previous Ukrainian strikes on Russian oil infrastructure, including facilities in occupied Crimea and western Russia. The Kyiv Independent reports Ukrainian forces have systematically targeted fuel storage and transport facilities as part of a strategy to degrade Russian logistics and revenue streams.

The strikes come amid broader Russian aerial attacks on Ukrainian infrastructure, including a recent strike on a civilian train station in Sumy Oblast.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy infrastructure has become a central front in the war. Ukraine is hitting Russian refining capacity and fuel logistics; Russia targets Ukrainian civilian infrastructure. The mutual targeting of energy systems reflects war fatigue on both sides — and the erosion of any energy deterrence norms.

sections.heading: Norway Overtakes Russia as World's Top Pipeline Gas Exporter
sections.body:
Norway became the world's largest pipeline gas exporter in 2025, overtaking Russia for the first time, according to MSN citing industry data. The shift represents the structural reordering of European gas supply: where Russia once supplied over 40% of EU gas via pipeline, Norway now fills that role.

The milestone reflects the permanent loss of Russian pipeline gas to Europe following the invasion of Ukraine and subsequent sanctions. Norway's Troll, Oseberg, and other North Sea fields have ramped production to compensate, with Norwegian gas now supplying Germany, the UK, and continental European markets.

For Oslo, the status comes with tradeoffs: accelerated production depletes reserves faster, and the Hormuz crisis has driven up LNG prices, making pipeline gas increasingly valuable for European buyers seeking supply security.
sections.whyItMatters: The Russia-to-Europe gas era is over. Norway is now Europe's primary pipeline supplier, with all the infrastructure investment and geopolitical dependency that entails. The shift is permanent.

sections.heading: Europe's e-SAF Sector Sees Hormuz-Driven Opportunity
sections.body:
Europe's struggling electro-Sustainable Aviation Fuel (e-SAF) sector is getting a boost from the Iran war, according to a France 24/AFP report. The Hormuz closure has squeezed jet fuel supplies, drawing attention to domestic synthetic fuel production as a security of supply issue.

'The conflict made the business case for e-SAF much stronger,' said Mariano Berkenwald, head of strategy at Ineratec, which operates Europe's only e-SAF facility in Frankfurt. The plant produces 2,500 tonnes annually — enough for about 50 transatlantic flights.

European military forces have expressed interest in e-SAF for fighter jets and helicopters, potentially paying a premium for domestic fuel security. E-SAF costs about 10 times more than kerosene, but its ingredients can be sourced entirely within Europe, avoiding maritime chokepoints entirely. The EU has mandated 1.2% SAF blending by 2030 and 35% by 2050.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy security is now driving industrial policy. e-SAF was a climate policy — it's becoming a defense priority. The question is whether crisis-driven attention translates into sustained investment.

sections.heading: UAE OPEC Exit Marks Structural Shift in Gulf Energy Politics
sections.body:
The United Arab Emirates formally left OPEC on May 1, 2026, ending nearly 60 years of membership, according to Malay Mail analysis. The exit frees Abu Dhabi from OPEC+ production quotas — but immediate output increases are constrained by Hormuz closure and war damage to UAE production facilities.

The departure stems from a $150 billion capacity expansion that pushed UAE production capability to 5 million bpd, well above its OPEC quota of 3.5 million bpd. RBC Capital Markets' Helima Croft noted Abu Dhabi had sought 'to monetise its investment in expanding capacity' — a desire that conflicted with Saudi-led production management.

The UAE becomes the largest oil producer ever to leave OPEC, joining the ranks of independent producers like the US and Brazil. Tensions with Saudi Arabia over conflicts in Sudan, Somalia, and Yemen contributed to the split, as did Abu Dhabi's closer ties to Washington and Tel Aviv.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ loses its fourth-largest producer at the worst possible time. The cartel's market management capacity shrinks just as global supply disruption from Hormuz peaks. Longer-term, the UAE exit weakens Gulf coordination and signals the erosion of Saudi-dominated oil diplomacy.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Negotiations: Deal Pending Trump Approval
sections.body:
US and Iranian negotiators have reached a tentative agreement to extend the ceasefire for 60 days and reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping, Reuters and Yahoo News report. The deal would also initiate nuclear talks. President Trump has not yet signed off on the proposal, with CBS News reporting he is 'in no hurry' to make a determination.

The White House previously pushed back on early reports of a deal, calling them 'propaganda.' Negotiators have reportedly made progress on the framework, but key details remain unresolved. US military strikes on Iranian targets continued during negotiations, with Iran's embassy in India blaming American sanctions for regional instability and defending its nuclear program as peaceful.

CNBC reports that even under a deal, oil exports may not return to pre-war levels immediately, as shipowners weigh risk assessments for Persian Gulf routes.
sections.whyItMatters: A Hormuz reopening would ease immediate supply concerns but leave the underlying architecture of vulnerability unchanged. The 60-day extension buys time for diplomacy — but time for what outcome remains unclear.

sections.heading: China-Japan Rare Earth Trade Strains Deepen
sections.body:
With China-Japan relations at a low point, Japanese industries dependent on rare earths fear shipments could slow or halt, the South China Morning Post reports. Beijing has already enacted limited export controls on critical minerals, and the lack of substantive bilateral communication channels has Japanese officials worried about further disruptions.

China's 2010 rare earth restriction followed a maritime incident near the disputed Diaoyu Islands, creating precedent for mineral weaponization. Now, with Beijing's export controls expanding and diplomatic dialogue stalled, 'practical communication regarding export controls and licensing procedures has become less transparent,' according to Kunihiko Shinoda of Japan's GRIPS.

The concerns come amid broader critical minerals coordination between India and the US under the Quad framework to reduce China dependence in key supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: Critical minerals are becoming leverage tools in regional disputes. Japan remembers 2010. With China controlling rare earth processing and relations deteriorating, supply chain diversification is accelerating — but alternatives take years to scale.

bottomLine: The energy architecture is in motion. Russia and China advanced the Power of Siberia-2 pipeline, locking in decades of overland gas trade. Ukraine hit Russian refineries and pipeline infrastructure. Norway cemented its role as Europe's primary gas supplier. The UAE left OPEC, weakening cartel coordination at the worst possible time. Europe began treating synthetic aviation fuel as a security priority. A US-Iran ceasefire deal awaits presidential approval. The common thread: energy infrastructure is no longer just economic — it's the primary terrain of great power competition.
sources: Reuters, El Pais, France 24/AFP, Malay Mail, MSN, Yahoo News, CBS News, CNBC, South China Morning Post, Firstpost, Kyiv Independent
